topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The clinking of champagne glasses was a relentless soundtrack to the reception. I kept my gaze fixed on the floral arrangements, anything to avoid the inevitable. Then, across the crowded dance floor, a familiar silhouette caught my eye. It was Sarah. She was laughing, radiant in a dress that seemed too bright for the evening, and the sight hit me like a physical jolt. My breath hitched, a shallow, almost silent intake of air that felt trapped in my chest. I immediately started fiddling with the strap of my clutch, my fingers tightening until the delicate leather creaked. Every nerve ending seemed to sharpen, pulling taut against my skin. I forced a smile, a brittle, practiced thing that felt like cracking plaster on my face, and began methodically navigating toward the periphery of the room, seeking a quiet corner where the noise couldn't reach me.

[story 2]

I was leaning against a pillar, nursing a lukewarm drink, trying to appear engrossed in a conversation about the appetizers. That’s when I saw him. Mark. He was standing near the buffet table, talking animatedly to someone, and the moment our eyes met, the room seemed to tilt. A coldness washed over me, a sudden drop in temperature that made the hairs on my arms stand up. My stomach clenched, a tight, nauseating knot forming beneath my ribs. I consciously shifted my weight, trying to look casual, but my hands felt clammy, slick with an unwelcome sweat. I mumbled something incoherent to the person next to me and abruptly excused myself, the sound of my own footsteps seeming deafeningly loud as I retreated toward the exit, needing space before I completely lost my footing.

[story 3]

The music was loud, a vibrant, overwhelming wave of sound washing over the ballroom. I was trying to observe the mingling guests, cataloging faces I knew and those I didn't, when I spotted him near the entrance. It was Liam. He looked… different. More polished, perhaps, or maybe just carrying a different kind of weight. A profound stillness settled over me, an internal vacuum where my usual energy used to be. I stopped mid-stride, my mouth suddenly dry. I watched him for a long moment, my eyes tracing the lines of his face, and a strange, hollow ache bloomed in my chest. I felt an intense urge to simply vanish, to dissolve into the background noise, so I quickly turned and walked toward the terrace doors, needing the cool night air to counteract the sudden internal heat.
